opp.name: PacificSeafood_SourcingMay'26
opp.next_step: 5.8.26 KC: Secure NDA Establish: an agenda and biz plan Deeper Discovery set for week of 5.25
opp.ais_why:
* The buyer showed real interest in the AI concept: he "Expressed enthusiasm for the concept" of a Teams-based conversational/AI-guided procurement assistant and autonomous bidding, and asked for a use-case-driven demo/trial.
* Enthusiasm is heavily gated by feasibility: "uncertain about the costs and ease of implementation," a legacy IBM AS/400 ERP with a "failed SAP S/4HANA" attempt, and "95% of processing facility staff lack digital proficiency."
* Early and ROI-focused: discussion centered on where AI could have realistic short-term impact (fragmented janitorial spend); next steps are a scoped demo and a sourcing ROI analysis - no POC or budget yet. Curious, not Hungry.

sources: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers: 'Janitorial supplies procurement is fragmented across locations; manual invoice tracking and price-per-square-foot analysis causes inefficiency'
Avoma 8 May, David: 'gathering all that data is a real pain in the ***'
Avoma 5 Jun demo, Rick: 'a lot of times will call the purchasing group and the purchasing group will make the purchases for them'
Avoma 5 Jun, David: 'I see a lot of benefit... would really make a lot of the stuff that we do easier and take a lot of stuff off our plate'
Avoma 5 Jun, Rick offered ROI workshop: 'our value engineering group will ask you about 50 questions... calculate what your potential value would be... based on our customers'
metrics.narrative: Business pain is confirmed but not quantified. David Brothers articulated two operational pain points on the 8 May discovery call: (1) 'Janitorial supplies procurement is fragmented across locations; manual invoice tracking and price-per-square-foot analysis causes inefficiency' — he described the current process as 'tracking down invoices and what we've been purchasing for the last year... gathering all that data is a real pain in the ***', and (2) broader procurement inefficiency where 'a lot of times will call the purchasing group and the purchasing group will make the purchases for them' (Rick, 5 Jun demo). The buyer has NOT quantified the cost of this inefficiency (FTE hours, error rate, savings opportunity, headcount savings). David acknowledged the value proposition ('I see a lot of benefit... would really make a lot of the stuff that we do easier and take a lot of stuff off our plate') but has not committed to measuring it. On 5 Jun Rick offered a sourcing ROI workshop ('our value engineering group will ask you about 50 questions and we'll start to calculate what your potential value would be... based on our customers') but David has not scheduled it. Gap: no baseline spend on janitorial supplies stated, no current cost-per-transaction or cycle time benchmarked, no hard savings target set. The next move is to run the ROI workshop with David and his VP Finance to quantify the efficiency gain and build the business case.

champion.narrative: No champion is secured; David Brothers is engaged but passive, not advocating internally, and he does not have access to the Economic Buyer. David is the sole buyer-side contact (Decision Maker role in Salesforce) and has participated in both calls (8 May discovery, 5 Jun demo). He has expressed genuine interest and understands the value ('I see a lot of benefit... we would benefit from this or something like this... definitely think we would benefit'), but he has not taken internal action to advance the deal. On 5 Jun he stated explicitly: 'I'm going to have to contemplate how to take this from this conversation to actually turning this into something actionable... this is definitely a long term conversation... there's a chance that we could turn into something that's really cool, there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say at this point.' This is a passive, exploratory posture, not championship. David has not introduced other stakeholders (no VP Finance, no GM Procurement, no IT lead has joined), has not requested a business case to present internally, and has not committed to next-step dates. He agreed to follow up ('let me think about how serious this starts to look, and then I'll follow up with you guys') but the ball is in his court with no forcing function. Evidence of advocacy: none. Evidence of multi-threading: none. Risk: David is a single point of contact at individual-contributor level with no executive sponsorship and no apparent urgency. The deal is at risk of going dark if David does not engage his leadership. The next move is to secure the discovery meeting, deliver the ROI business case, and ask David to introduce the VP Finance or GM Procurement so we can multi-thread and reach the budget authority.

sources: Avoma 8 May, David: 'I think I also talked to a company called Zip'
Avoma 8 May, David: 'I'm not comparing you guys to Zip really... I still want to give you the impression that '
Avoma 5 Jun, David: 'there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say at this point'
Avoma 5 Jun, Rick: positioned single-code-base architecture, agentic AI, Microsoft Teams integration as differentiators
Avoma 5 Jun, David: 'I like it. I think it sounds great' (positive response to Teams demo)
competition.narrative: No named competitor has been mentioned by the buyer, but competitive context is present. On 8 May David stated 'I think I also talked to a company called Zip' (ZipHQ), indicating he is conducting some vendor evaluation. When Rick began positioning Zycus's differentiation against Zip, David cut him off: 'I'm not comparing you guys to Zip really... I still want to give you the impression that.' David's emphasis was on understanding implementation feasibility, not feature comparison, suggesting he is early in the evaluation and has not formalized a shortlist. No other procurement platforms (Coupa, SAP Ariba, GEP, Jaggaer, Ivalua) were named. David's question 'is this something that's kind of an enterprise level system or a handful of people are using this' suggests he may be considering lighter-weight solutions or a phased internal build vs a full platform. The 'do nothing' alternative is a real threat given David's caution: 'there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say at this point.' Rick positioned Zycus's single-code-base architecture, agentic AI, and Microsoft Teams integration as differentiators, and David responded positively to the Teams demo ('I like it. I think it sounds great'), which is a wedge. Gap: we do not know if Pacific Seafood is running a formal RFP, whether Zip or others are finalists, or what the buyer's evaluation timeline is. The next move is to ask David directly during the discovery meeting whether he is evaluating other vendors and what his decision timeline is.

sources: Avoma 8 May, David Brothers: 'Janitorial supplies procurement is fragmented across locations; manual invoice tracking and price-per-square-foot analysis causes inefficiency'
Avoma 8 May, David: 'tracking down the invoices and what we've been purchasing for the last year... gathering all that data is a real pain in the ***'
Avoma 5 Jun, Rick: 'a lot of times will call the purchasing group and the purchasing group will make the purchases for them'
Avoma 5 Jun, David: 'I see a lot of benefit... take a lot of stuff off our plate that we don't need to have on our plate'

Avoma 8 May, David: 'implementation within Pacific Seafood is extremely difficult for enterprise level technology... we're the seafood industry... very technologically unsophisticated people... 95% lack digital proficiency; many don't use email'
identify_pain.narrative: Operational pain is confirmed and well-articulated by David Brothers, the pain owner. On 8 May David described two specific pain points owned by his indirect procurement team: (1) Fragmented, manual janitorial supplies procurement across multiple Pacific Seafood sites — 'Janitorial supplies procurement is fragmented across locations; manual invoice tracking and price-per-square-foot analysis causes inefficiency... tracking down the invoices and what we've been purchasing for the last year... gathering all that data is a real pain in the ***... then presenting it to our people who are trying to bid for the business'. This is a recurring, high-frequency pain affecting David's team directly. (2) Reactive, low-value transactional work where end-users call the procurement team for every purchase instead of self-serving — Rick summarized this on 5 Jun: 'a lot of times will call the purchasing group and the purchasing group will make the purchases for them. Boy, wouldn't it be nice if they could do it all?' David agreed: 'I see a lot of benefit... take a lot of stuff off our plate that we don't need to have on our plate'. David also raised a third, broader pain: implementation complexity and change-management risk in a low-digital-maturity organization — 'implementation within Pacific Seafood is extremely difficult for enterprise level technology... we're the seafood industry... very technologically unsophisticated people... 95% lack digital proficiency; many don't use email... if you go out on the coast where our big processing facilities are, we really need the most help. You're looking at people who, for generations, have lived and worked on, and this is an entire culture of people here that are really not even doing emails. So implementation is gonna be very difficult.' This is a strategic constraint and a core evaluation criterion (see Decision Criteria). The pain is confirmed and owned by David; it is not yet quantified in cost or FTE terms.

sources: Salesforce Next_Step (~8 May): 'Secure NDA' (not yet executed, no follow-up logged)
Avoma 8 May, David: 'we're still operating AS/400... we had a failed SAP S/4HANA attempt 2 years ago... we are working towards a new ERP solution... we don't have a definite timeline on that ERP yet'
Avoma 8 May, Rick: 'ERP wise, we've got experience with connecting our solution to a number of them... we have APIs'
Avoma 5 Jun, Rick: licensing model walkthrough (user count-based, annual subscription)
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, and InfoSec requirements are unknown. The Salesforce Next_Step (~8 May) included 'Secure NDA' as the first action item, but no NDA has been executed and no follow-up on it appears in the evidence. No MSA, SOW, or Order Form discussion has occurred. No InfoSec questionnaire, security review, or ERP integration scoping has been mentioned. No SI/partner involvement has been raised. On 8 May David described Pacific Seafood's ERP environment ('we're still operating AS/400... we had a failed SAP S/4HANA attempt 2 years ago... we are working towards a new ERP solution... we don't have a definite timeline on that ERP yet') and Rick confirmed Zycus connects via APIs to common ERP systems, but no integration deep-dive or technical review has been scheduled. On 5 Jun Rick walked through the licensing model (user count-based, annual subscription) but no pricing, terms, or contract structure were finalized. Gap: we do not know Pacific Seafood's procurement approval process (is this a direct buy, does it go through a sourcing RFP, is legal review required for SaaS contracts), whether InfoSec must approve cloud vendors, what the contracting timeline is, or whether implementation services will be scoped under an SOW. The next step is the discovery meeting, during which the deal team should ask explicitly about procurement policy, legal/InfoSec gates, and contracting authority.

economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is not identified and has not participated in any conversation. David Brothers holds the Decision Maker contact role and is the sole buyer-side contact, but he is an 'Internal spend Category Coordinator' (individual contributor level) and is not the budget authority. On the 5 Jun demo call, pricing and cost were raised multiple times by David — he asked 'what are we looking at here... it's one thing if I'm bringing in a $2M annual project, that's a completely different conversation than if it's $50k annual' and 'I don't fully understand the cost' — but no VP Finance, CFO, or procurement director joined to own the commercial conversation. Rick mentioned 'VP of Finance' once as a hypothetical user persona ('it's helping us organize it and interact with our VP of finance') but that person never appeared. David himself stated 'I'm going to have to contemplate how to take this from this conversation to actually turning this into something actionable', signaling he does not control the budget approval. The Salesforce Next_Step from ~8 May names no EB engagement plan. Gap: we do not know who approves a $110k software spend at Pacific Seafood, whether CFO sign-off is required, or what the approval threshold is. The account-level contact list includes a 'VP Sales / Procurement' (Steve Spencer) and 'GM Central Procurement' (Doug Roan) who may hold budget authority but neither has been introduced or engaged. The next move is to ask David directly who approves this spend and request an intro to that person for the ROI workshop and pricing conversation.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is informal and not yet defined. The call sequence shows David Brothers as the sole buyer participant across both the 8 May discovery call and the 5 Jun demo; no other Pacific Seafood stakeholders have joined, and no multi-person evaluation committee or approval chain has been referenced. David stated 5 Jun 'I'm going to have to contemplate how to take this from this conversation to actually turning this into something actionable', indicating he does not yet have a plan to advance the deal internally. He described himself as a 'long term prospect' and acknowledged 'there's a chance that we could turn into something that's really cool, there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say at this point'. This is a single-threaded, exploratory conversation with no formal vendor evaluation, no shortlist, and no timeline. The Salesforce Next_Step (~8 May) planned 'Secure NDA, Establish agenda and biz plan, Deeper Discovery set for week of 5.25', but the 25 May discovery meeting is now overdue with no follow-up logged. On 5 Jun Rick proposed next steps: (1) a 30-45 minute discovery meeting to capture procurement pains, (2) a follow-on demo tailored to Pacific Seafood's use cases, and (3) an invitation to the Zycus customer event in Colorado (September 2026) to network with peer companies. David agreed in principle ('yeah, let me think about how serious this starts to look, and then I'll follow up with you guys') but has not committed to dates. Gap: we do not know if Pacific Seafood is running a competitive evaluation, whether an RFP will be issued, who else must approve, or what the buyer's internal timeline is. The next move is to secure the discovery meeting and map the approval chain.

decision_criteria.narrative: Partial evaluation criteria have emerged from the two calls, but no formal RFP, BRD, or written decision framework has been shared. David's stated priorities (8 May and 5 Jun calls) are: (1) Implementation simplicity and speed — he emphasized multiple times that 'implementation within Pacific Seafood is extremely difficult for enterprise level technology' and asked 'how complex is it to do a demo... how easy that would be' and 'is this something that's kind of an enterprise level system or a handful of people are using this'. He is explicitly NOT looking for an enterprise-wide ERP-class rollout; he wants a solution his indirect procurement team (a handful of people) can adopt quickly without a large change-management lift. (2) Microsoft Teams integration and user-friendliness — David requested twice 'could you show us Teams... I'd love to see that' and responded positively to the natural-language interface demo ('I like it. I think it sounds great'). (3) Cost and ROI clarity — David stated 5 Jun 'I don't fully understand the cost... help me understand what kind of conversation I would be having' and asked for 'some general idea' to determine scale. (4) Proven fit for a 'very old world industry' with low digital maturity — David contextualized 8 May: 'we're the seafood industry... it's still a very old world industry... you're dealing with very technologically unsophisticated people... 95% lack digital proficiency; many don't use email'. He is evaluating whether Zycus can work in that environment. No formal scorecard, no competitive bake-off criteria, and no multi-stakeholder evaluation committee has been mentioned. The next step is a discovery meeting ('we'll ask you a number of questions... mostly around your pains and challenges', Rick 5 Jun) to formalize use cases and capture formal requirements.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS score or status is recorded in Salesforce (AIS fields are null). Based on the buyer's language and engagement, Pacific Seafood is AI Curious but cautious: David expressed interest in AI-driven automation and the Merlin Teams interface ('I like it... sounds really cool') but emphasized implementation feasibility and cost over AI innovation. His organization is low digital maturity ('very technologically unsophisticated people... 95% lack digital proficiency'), so AI appetite is present but secondary to ease-of-use and change-management concerns. The AI positioning is landing as a user-experience benefit (Teams, natural language), not a strategic differentiator.

items.detail: No Economic Buyer identified or engaged; David Brothers (IC-level Decision Maker) does not control $110k budget approval and has not introduced VP Finance, GM Procurement, or C-level sponsor. Single-threaded through one passive contact with no executive sponsorship is a ghost risk.

Cost and ROI not quantified; David requested 'some general idea of what I'm looking at... it's one thing if I'm bringing in a $2M annual project, that's a completely different conversation than if it's $50k annual'. No business case has been built to justify the $110k spend to his leadership or budget authority.

Implementation complexity and change-management risk are major buyer concerns in a low-digital-maturity organization. David stated 8 May: 'implementation within Pacific Seafood is extremely difficult for enterprise level technology... we're the seafood industry... very technologically unsophisticated people... 95% lack digital proficiency; many don't use email... we had a failed SAP S/4HANA attempt 2 years ago... implementation is gonna be very difficult'. Buyer fears large-scale rollout.

No buyer timeline, urgency, or compelling event. David positioned as 'long term prospect' with no internal deadline. Discovery meeting originally set for week of 25 May is now 1 month overdue with no follow-up. Deal is drifting.

confidence_signals.summary: Deal is live but cooling. Two buyer calls in 4 weeks (8 May, 5 Jun) show genuine interest, but no forward activity since 17 Jun (9 days ago) and the planned 25 May discovery meeting is overdue. David is interested but passive; he has not introduced other stakeholders, not scheduled the next meeting, and left the ball in his court ('I'll follow up with you guys'). Single-threaded through an IC-level contact with no executive sponsorship and no urgency is a classic stall pattern. Confidence: Medium, trending toward Low if no re-engagement by end of June.

north_star_verdict.math: 140 days to close (13 Nov 2026). Deal created 8 May 2026, currently 49 days old. Stage: Qualified. Time in Qualified: ~49 days since creation. Last buyer call 5 Jun (~3 weeks ago). Last Salesforce activity 17 Jun (9 days ago). The close date is 4.5 months out, but the deal is only in Qualified stage with discovery still underway, no EB identified, no champion secured, no pricing discussion completed, no legal/contracting begun. Typical enterprise cycle is 12-15 months; this deal would need to complete discovery, shortlisting, vendor selection, negotiation, and contracting in under 5 months. Pace required: move to Shortlisted within 30 days, Vendor Selected within 60 days, and close contracting in the remaining 80 days. Current pace: two buyer calls in 4 weeks, discovery meeting completed 5 Jun with next step set for week of 25 May (already past), no forward movement logged since 17 Jun. The deal is fundamentally healthy and engaged, but the date is a placeholder, not a credible forecast.

north_star_verdict.headline: Two engaged buyer calls in 7 weeks show genuine interest, but deal stuck in early Qualified stage with no EB mapped, no champion, and massive implementation fear. David is interested but passive; 140-day close requires aggressive acceleration from current glacial.

best_practice_check.summary: Three strategic levers to raise win probability: (1) Secure executive sponsorship to convert David's interest into budget and urgency. (2) Run the ROI workshop to quantify the efficiency gain and arm David with a business case. (3) Multi-thread beyond David to reach the VP Finance or GM Procurement who controls the $110k spend approval.

competitive_position.summary: No formalized competitive evaluation is underway, but ZipHQ is a known alternative David has spoken with. David explicitly de-emphasized vendor comparison on 8 May ('I'm not comparing you guys to Zip really') and focused instead on internal feasibility, suggesting competition is secondary to the 'do nothing' risk at this stage. Zycus's Microsoft Teams integration is a confirmed wedge (David requested the demo twice and responded positively). The single strongest current threat is the 'do nothing' alternative driven by implementation fear and lack of executive sponsorship — David's caution ('there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say') and his emphasis on Pacific Seafood's low digital maturity and failed SAP implementation signal high inertia. No evidence of a live bake-off, RFP, or finalist shortlist. How we win: prove fast time-to-value with a scoped indirect procurement team pilot (not enterprise-wide), deliver a quantified ROI business case tied to David's janitorial-supplies pain, secure executive sponsorship (VP Finance or GM Procurement) to create urgency and budget approval, and leverage the Teams integration as the user-adoption wedge in a low-digital-maturity environment.

competitors.how_we_win: Zycus offers full end-to-end platform (sourcing, contracts, spend analysis, P2P) vs Zip's narrower iSource-only scope; position the single-code-base architecture and agentic AI negotiation agents as differentiation; leverage Microsoft Teams integration (David requested demo twice and liked it) as the user-adoption wedge in Pacific Seafood's low-digital-maturity environment

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI / Merlin positioning was strong and well-received during the demo. Rick led with agentic AI capabilities (Merlin intake, autonomous negotiation agents, agent-to-agent communication across modules) and demonstrated the Microsoft Teams natural-language interface twice at David's request. David responded positively ('I like it. I think it sounds great') and the Teams integration became a confirmed wedge. The AI narrative is landing. No evidence of under-positioning.

items.quote: could you show us Teams... I'd love to see that... I like it. I think it sounds great
items.source: Avoma 5 Jun demo call, David Brothers
items.criterion: Microsoft Teams integration and ease of use

items.quote: implementation within Pacific Seafood is extremely difficult for enterprise level technology... is this something that's kind of an enterprise level system or a handful of people are using this
items.source: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers
items.criterion: Implementation simplicity and speed for a small procurement team (not enterprise-wide)

items.quote: I don't fully understand the cost... help me understand what kind of conversation I would be having... it's one thing if I'm bringing in a $2M annual project, that's a completely different conversation than if it's $50k annual
items.source: Avoma 5 Jun demo call, David Brothers

items.quote: we're the seafood industry... very technologically unsophisticated people... 95% lack digital proficiency; many don't use email... implementation is gonna be very difficult
items.source: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers
items.criterion: Fit for a low-digital-maturity, traditional industry organization

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Pacific Seafood's expectations are aligned with Zycus's capabilities on user experience (Teams integration, natural language interface) but exposed on implementation complexity and cost clarity. David's core ask is a solution that his small indirect procurement team can adopt quickly without enterprise-wide change management in a low-digital-maturity organization.

hard.ais_why:
* The buyer showed real interest in the AI concept: he "Expressed enthusiasm for the concept" of a Teams-based conversational/AI-guided procurement assistant and autonomous bidding, and asked for a use-case-driven demo/trial.
* Enthusiasm is heavily gated by feasibility: "uncertain about the costs and ease of implementation," a legacy IBM AS/400 ERP with a "failed SAP S/4HANA" attempt, and "95% of processing facility staff lack digital proficiency."
* Early and ROI-focused: discussion centered on where AI could have realistic short-term impact (fragmented janitorial spend); next steps are a scoped demo and a sourcing ROI analysis - no POC or budget yet. Curious, not Hungry.

deltas.subject: Single-threaded through an individual contributor with no executive sponsorship — David Brothers (Internal spend Category Coordinator) is the sole buyer-side contact and does not control the budget; no VP Finance, GM Procurement, or C-level sponsor has been engaged. In an enterprise deal with a 12-15 month cycle and $110k spend, relying on one IC-level contact is a ghost risk. Next move: ask David to introduce his VP Finance (Steve Spencer or the finance leader) for the ROI workshop and multi-thread to the GM Central Procurement (Doug Roan, account-level contact) to map the approval chain and create executive-level sponsorship.

Overdue next step with no forward momentum since 17 Jun — the Salesforce Next_Step set ~8 May planned 'Deeper Discovery set for week of 5.25', which is now more than 1 month overdue, and the last Salesforce activity was 17 Jun (9 days ago) with no logged buyer response. David positioned himself as a 'long term prospect' and left the ball in his court ('I'll follow up with you guys'), but passive follow-up in an early-stage, single-threaded deal is a stall pattern. Next move: the deal owner (Rick Taranek) should reach out directly this week (by 28 Jun) with a specific discovery meeting invite (propose 2-3 dates, week of 1 Jul) and attach the draft ROI estimate and the promised Showpad materials to create a forcing function and re-engage David.
